responseSet.title: Forensic follow-up care for outpatients in Germany
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Forensic follow-up care for outpatients in Germany

Markus Feil

Markus Feil

Munich
Germany

Markus Feil   *Email: mfeil@hilfswerk-muenchen.de
forensic psychotherapy, supervision of conduct, forensic follow-up care

Legislation has revived forensic psychotherapy for outpatients in Germany. Since 2007, in almost all 16 federal states of Germany forensic outpatient clinics have been and are being established for criminal and sex offenders who have been released from prison or secure detention under conditions imposed by the court. This paper reviews the consequences of this legislation for forensic outpatients, and will focus on some of the central organisational, institutional and social aspects that stem from the experience of the author. Although the conclusions may not be new, they require repeated reflection and well-considered political commitment.

responseSet.title: Creativity and psychotic states in exceptional people: the work of Murray Jackson
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creativity and psychotic states in exceptional people: the work of Murray Jackson

R. Hinshelwood

R. Hinshelwood

Emeritus Professor, Centre for Psychoanalytic Studies

R. Hinshelwood   *Email: rhinsh@essex.ac.uk

This has been a book waiting to be written, a psychoanalyst’s observations on instances when genius is akin to madness. In fact, it was unfinished when Murray Jackson died in 2011, and so it had indeed to wait; Jeanne Magagna subsequently put the text together, and added connecting and clarifying parts. Here are four case histories arrived at from published sources of men (no women yet) who have the highest honours, even at the Nobel level, and who remained vulnerable to the worst of mental illness. Magagna has collected these four ‘cases’, and has contributed editorially with Jackson and substantively. And she starts with a summary of the specific features which repeat themselves through the four.

Murray Jackson is well known for his humane writings that get benignly under the skin of psychiatric patients. Here, he follows a model used by Freud in the ‘analysis’ of Judge Schreber, the use of published texts to explore in a speculative way the core of the person’s terrible problems. In Freud’s case, this gave rise to fruitful additions to his theories, despite the fact he never met the patient outside the published text. Not least was the formulation of the reality principle. The present title suggests we will be introduced to a possible correlation of creativity and psychotic states of mind.

We meet the four only from their appearance in various biographical and autobiographical texts. But we do have the benefit of Jackson’s intuitive reading between the lines, and exposing the ‘psychotic core’; he places his eye on this ‘psychotic wavelength’, as Richard Lucas called it. He shows us the childhood experiences of John Nash, Vaslav Nijinsky, Jose Saramago and Vincent Van Gogh in a new light. He shows first their creation of a new reality; it is their psychotically imagined one – psychotically believed. Jackson lights up the nightmare world, which these tortured people actually see in those around them, in the places, events and relationships of ordinary life. The point is that for these individuals, the nightmare is above all not within them. It is displayed in lived form all around. It is Jackson’s gift to pick out the originating features of this nightmare landscape as it remains alive in the individual’s real world not – and no longer – within him. 115

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

Jessica Yakeley   *Email: jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

It is with great pleasure that I welcome Dr. Paul Cundy as our new Assistant Editor. Paul is an experienced Clinical Psychologist currently working in the City & Hackney Primary Care Psychotherapy Consultation Service. His knowledge of psychoanalytic psychotherapy, as well as both quantitative and qualitative research methodology, makes him ideally qualified for this role.

The papers and book reviews in this Spring issue of the journal demonstrate the pluralistic nature of contemporary psychoanalytic psychotherapy in which the use of different research methods and development of different treatment modalities may advance our understanding of patients’ experiences and responses to our therapeutic endeavours. Indeed, plurality and research methodology in psychoanalysis constitute the focus of our first paper ‘Pluralistic approaches to the study of process and outcome in psychoanalysis: The LAC Depression Study’ by Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber, Lisa Kallenbach and Margerete Schoett. In defining psychoanalysis as a specific scientific discipline of the unconscious, this paper explores the many different forms of research that have emerged in the last 100 years in psychoanalysis. The authors differentiate between two broad groups of psychoanalytic research: clinical research, meaning genuine psychoanalytic research in the psychoanalytic situation itself; and extra-clinical research, which occurs after the psychoanalytic sessions, embraces a variety of research strategies, and includes empirical, experimental and interdisciplinary studies. The authors illustrate their ideas with reference to a major on-going study on depression at the Sigmund Freud Institute in Frankfurt, which employs different research methodologies including a randomised controlled trial comparing psychoanalytic treatment with cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT) in the treatment of chronically depressed patients.

The theme of research into the treatment of depression with psychoanalytically informed psychotherapies continues into the next two papers. In ‘Trauma, personality disorders and chronic depression – the role of the conversational model of psychodynamic psychotherapy in treatment resistant depression’, Janine Stevenson, Joan Haliburn and Shaun Halovic describe their adaptation in Australia of the conversational model of therapy, originally developed by Robert Hobson and Russell Meares in the UK in the 1960s from a psychoanalytic framework. In a preliminary study of the effectiveness of this treatment approach, Stevenson and colleagues found that patients with treatment resistant depression showed significant improvement in symptoms, self-esteem, functioning and suicidality after 12 months twice weekly therapy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley and Gill McGauley

Gill McGauley

London
UK

London
UK

Gill McGauley  

The field of forensic psychotherapy may appear to be comprised of uneasy coalitions between disciplines and paradigms with diverse and sometimes opposing principles – therapeutics and the law, forensic psychiatry and psychoanalysis, moral ethics and scientific empiricism. However, its distinct identity arises from its efforts to maintain a dialectic between the tensions and conflicts existing at its core, from which creativity and innovation may emerge. In this second volume of our Special Issue on Forensic Psychotherapy we offer an international perspective, with papers from the United States and Germany, as well as the UK, illustrating how these dilemmas are explored and addressed in different political, sociological and cultural contexts.

How can Freud’s ideas, originating in his discoveries of treating hysterical women in late-nineteenth and early twentieth-century Vienna, be applied to the forensic world of murderers and rapists? How can psychoanalysis, which traditionally considers antisocial and violent behaviour as serious contradictions for psychoanalytic treatment, be adapted to the therapeutic needs of offenders? James Gilligan, one of the foremost contemporary psychoanalytic thinkers on violence, proposes that psychoanalysis offers the most powerful cognitive and emotional understanding of the workings of the human psyche than any other method, shifting the philosophical and moral question ‘How should I live and what should I do?’ to the scientific question ‘How can I live?’ i.e. ‘What processes maintain life, and which forces lead to damage and death?’ He asserts that these questions can be empirically examined and tested by applying psychoanalytic theories and methods to the study of human behaviours, including those of extreme violence.

Gilligan relates his own personal and professional journey, starting as a recent graduate from Harvard Medical School taking a job as a prison psychiatrist to support his young family, where he discovered to his surprise that working with convicted criminals and murderers, who had been deemed untreatable sociopaths deserving only punishment and control, was one of the most emotionally moving experiences of his career to date. In this and his subsequent work in the Massachusetts prison system, where he eventually became the director of mental health services, he discovered that far from being amoral, as was widely believed, murderers and rapists were preoccupied with fundamental questions of morality. Through his work with high-security inmates, he found that their early experiences of being rejected, ostracised, abused or made to feel as if they did not exist, predisposed them as adults to be sensitised to feeling bullied, ignored or ‘disrespected’, leading to unbearable feelings of shame and humiliation which need to be defended against by violent means. [[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

Jessica Yakeley   *Email: jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

As I write this at the beginning of July, the United Kingdom is reeling from the results of the vote to leave the European Union. Amidst the political uncertainty and economic limbo, one question is foremost for many of us: What will be the future of our National Health Service (NHS) and other public services which are already struggling with insufficient resources and the relentless pressure to reorganise and become more ‘efficient’? As history accelerates with political allegiances, institutional structures and media opinions changing so rapidly, it seems to me that the psychoanalytic attitude offers a degree of psychic stability in its understanding of these momentous events. Psychoanalysis has always been subversive, on the fringes of mainstream debate, but quietly challenging our notions of free will and conscious choice. Brexit can be seen through the psychoanalytic lens as representing a fundamental breach in our epistemic trust of society, exposing the mayhem of the collective unconscious running riot in the absence of an adequate container.

The papers in this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy reveal the paradox of psychoanalytic thinking: by the exposition of unconscious truths which disturb the status quo, new understandings of the individual, institution or society may emerge which enhance resilience and provide the necessary containment to survive and grow. This is aptly illustrated in the paper by James Johnston, Vikram Luthra, Lackson Mzizi and Alastair Cardno – ‘Medical psychotherapy consultation: Psychoanalytic psychiatry for the patient and professional’. This describes how a medical psychotherapy consultation service informed by psychoanalytic practice has developed to offer help to acute and community mental health teams in an NHS trust in thinking about patients with whom the teams feel stuck. Johnston and his colleagues have developed a model of consultation that they call a ‘psychoanalytically informed sandwich’: the bread is the meeting with professionals with a particular focus on countertransference, and the patient is the filling, whom the consulting medical psychotherapist may or may not meet (‘meat’) depending on whether they think it would be in the patient’s best interest. The provision of new food for thought facilitates the freeing of adhesive identifications between patient and professional, described as the ‘grievance glue’ that holds together these stuck relationships. The authors also provide an evaluation of their service in which, over a seven-year period, they sampled a significant proportion of consultations to determine the demographic and diagnostic profiles and outcomes of the consultation process, as well as conducting an online survey 201

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK
Tavistock and Portman NHS Foundation Trust

Jessica Yakeley   *Email: jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, with papers from Germany, the UK and Italy, explores from a psychoanalytic perspective the concepts of trauma, depression and personality disorder, the relationships between them, and the limitations of conventional diagnostic categories. The first paper, ‘Childhood trauma from a patient and a psychoanalyst perspective: linking chronic depression to relational multiple trauma’ by Alexa Negele, Johannes Kaufhold and Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber, examines the relationship between multiple childhood trauma, adversity and chronic depression. This is the second paper published in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy by researchers from the LAC depression study, a major, multi-site, ongoing comparative study of outcomes of psychoanalytical and cognitive behavioural long-term treatments of chronically depressed patients conducted at the Sigmund Freud Institute in Frankfurt (see Leuzinger-Bohleber, Kallenbach, & Schoett, 2016). Findings from the LAC study showed that three quarters of participating patients reported clinically relevant histories of childhood trauma. This paper examines a subsample of patients in the larger LAC study, comparing patient self-ratings of trauma with their treating psychoanalysts’ perspectives, the results of which support the connection between multiple childhood trauma and chronic depression. These findings are illustrated with detailed clinical material from one of the patients who participated in the LAC study from the beginning of his therapy to five-year follow-up, which convincingly demonstrates how the patient was able to gain insight into the effects that his childhood traumatic experiences had on his adult relationships and mental health due to a psychoanalytic therapeutic process in which both the patient and the analyst worked together to reconstruct the meaning of traumatic experiences. The emerging findings from LAC study complement the results of the Tavistock Adult Depression Study (TADS) (Fonagy et al., 2015) which showed the efficacy of psychoanalytic psychotherapy in patients with treatment-resistant depression, and are of crucial importance in demonstrating to influential advisory bodies such as the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) that psychoanalytic psychotherapy has been empirically shown to be effective for depression.

[[RunningHead]]

Trauma, personality disorders and chronic depression – the role of the conversational model of psychodynamic psychotherapy in treatment resistant depression

Janine Stevenson , Joan Haliburn and Shaun Halovic

Shaun Halovic

Wentworthville
Australia
Department of Psychiatry

North Parramatta
Australia
Mental Health Sciences Centre

NSW
Australia
Department of Psychological Medicine

Shaun Halovic  

treatment-resistant depression, Conversational Model, psychodynamic psychotherapy, personality disorders, trauma, comorbidity

Objective: To report preliminary data describing the interim treatment outcome of 44 patients referred with treatment-resistant depression (TRD), comorbid personality disorders and histories of early childhood trauma using the Conversational Model (CM) of psychodynamic psychotherapy. Method: Patients (N = 44), 13 males and 31 females with long histories of depression ranging from 2 to 30 years, resistant to multiple trials of treatment, were referred by mental health practitioners, including psychiatrists. They were treated with twice weekly CM psychotherapy by multidisciplinary trainees and supervised by experienced trained clinicians. Questionnaires were administered at assessment and at 12 months to assess symptoms, functioning, self-esteem, history of trauma, personality functioning and suicidality. In this preliminary study, there was no separate control group, and patients served as their own controls. Results: Patients with TRD were found to have comorbid severe personality disorders and histories of early childhood trauma. Significant improvement in symptoms, self-esteem, functioning and suicidality was noted after 12 months. Conclusion: Patients responded with symptomatic and functional improvement to twice weekly CM therapy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Othering, blame and shame when working with people living with HIV

Poul Rohleder

Poul Rohleder

London
UK
Professional Doctorate in Clinical Psychology, School of Psychology

Poul Rohleder   *Email: P.A.Rohleder@uel.ac.uk
HIV, stigma, othering, shame, sexuality

Significant advances in HIV treatment has meant that for the majority of patients with HIV, they are able to live a normal lifespan. However, HIV remains a highly stigmatizing disease with the potential to significantly impact on one’s social identity and sense of self. This paper draws on data from a qualitative study of interviews with five gay men, to explore the experiences of shame in relation to living with HIV. The paper adopts a psychoanalytic lens to highlight the mechanisms of splitting that may be involved at both a social and individual level, and the experience of shame among the participants. The paper aims to use this research data to supplement our understanding of what may be occurring ‘on the couch’ with patients who are living with HIV.

[[RunningHead]]

The Balint group experience for medical students: a pilot project

Sheila O’Neill , Kirsty Foster and Alexa Gilbert-Obrart

Alexa Gilbert-Obrart

Sydney
Australia
Sydney Medical School Northern & The Kolling Institute

Sydney
Australia

Wollongong
Australia
Graduate School of Medicine

Alexa Gilbert-Obrart  

Balint group, medical students, doctor–patient relationship, group dynamics, empathy

Loss of empathy has been reported in medical students as they move through the clinical phases of their training. Several researchers have attempted to address this issue by exploring ways of heightening students’ awareness of the emotional, non-biomedical aspects of illness and the dynamics of the doctor–patient relationship, using a variety of reflective group discussion methods. This pilot project employed the specific group method developed by Michael Balint for general practitioners working in London after the Second World War. The pilot was based on one group of six third-year graduate students, meeting weekly over six weeks. Evaluation includes pre- and post-questionnaires, a 1000-word essay and leaders’ observations. The results suggest that the traditional Balint method needs to be modified for students at a point in their training where they have not yet been exposed to patients for long enough to develop meaningful patient relationships. Nevertheless, there was some evidence of a heightened awareness of the dynamics of doctor–patient relationships and the importance of psychological/emotional factors (including their own prejudices) when interacting with a patient. Balint-style groups could be an effective way of encouraging medical students to reflect on the importance of emotions in the doctor–patient relationship.

responseSet.title: Journeys in psychoanalysis: the selected work of Elizabeth Spillius
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journeys in psychoanalysis: the selected work of Elizabeth Spillius

Stanley Ruszczynski

Stanley Ruszczynski

London
UK

Stanley Ruszczynski   *Email: sruszczynski@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Elizabeth Spillius writes with such lucidity and with such depth and range of knowledge that, prior to her death in July 2016, she was established as one of the finest proponents and champions not only of clinical psychoanalytic theory and practice, but of thinking psychoanalytically about whatever matters she addressed.

This collection is presented in the Routledge ‘World Library of Mental Health’ series which sets out to celebrate important contributions to thinking about mental health made by leading experts in their fields. Others in the series include Colin Murray Parkes and Jeremy Holmes.

The aim of this volume is to represent the range of Spillius’ thinking and writing, and therefore warrants, in my view, including in this book, 6 of the 13 papers already published in the 2007 collection ‘Encounters with Melanie Klein: Selected Papers of Elizabeth Spillius’ edited by Priscilla Roth and Richard Rusbridger (Routledge, 2007).

Spillius was a successful anthropologist (represented by the first chapter in this book, on conjugal roles and social networks) before she trained as a psychoanalyst. What emerges in the papers in this book is evidence of the continuation of Spillius as anthropologist as well as Spillius as psychoanalyst, heavily influenced by her reading and in-depth knowledge of the work of Melanie Klein. In her paper ‘On becoming a British Psychoanalyst’, she refers to herself ‘looking at the (British Psychoanalytic) society with an anthropologist’s eye’ (p. 148). I suggest that in her writings she casts her anthropologist’s eye more widely than that and in fact looks at psychoanalysis as a whole, and related matters, with her anthropologist’s eye.

Anthropology might be defined as the study of cultures, social structures and the nature of interpersonal relationships, and their development over time. Spillius has conducted such an exploration of a number of aspects of psychoanalysis and some of its socio-political–theoretical history, including delving into the Melanie Klein Archives held in the Wellcome Library.

responseSet.title: Murdered father, dead father: revisiting the Oedipus Complex
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Murdered father, dead father: revisiting the Oedipus Complex

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

Jessica Yakeley   *Email: jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This is a deeply analytic book. By this, I mean that its author not only revisits and expands our understanding of the core concepts of psychoanalysis that form the foundation of the discipline, but that her analysis of these concepts shows a depth of insight and offers new understandings and interpretations of Freud’s work.

Rosine Perelberg is one of the foremost psychoanalysts of the British Psychoanalytic Society today. She has an international reputation for her scholarly exploration of Freud’s texts in relation to contemporary concerns including femininity, violence and trauma, and has published widely on these topics. This latest book builds on her long-standing interest in a psychoanalytic understanding of time, memory and space, and she vividly illustrates her ideas with clinical vignettes of patients in psychoanalytic treatment, literary references, anthropological theories, and reference to important historical events. She also explores and incorporates psychoanalytic concepts and theories from the French psychoanalytic tradition, which is heavily influenced by Lacan’s works, but with which much of the Anglo-American psychoanalytic community remains unfamiliar. Many of the ideas she presents are complex and assume a prior familiarity with Freud’s works, which may deter readers less versed in the details of Freudian theory. Nevertheless, I would urge such readers to persevere, and the book’s glossary of psychoanalytic concepts is very helpful in this respect.

Perelberg starts with a re-examination of the theme of killing of the father, which recurs throughout Freud’s work in various guises. She makes a crucial distinction between what she calls the ‘murdered father’ and the ‘dead father’. The former refers to an omnipotent narcissistic phantasy in which, through killing the father, the person occupies the place of the father/phallus who possesses all women and tyrannizes his sons in an imaginary/dyadic/anal-sadistic world. The dead father, by contrast, more powerful than the real father, constitutes the symbolic order and institution of the law – this is the paternal function. The shift from the murdered father to the dead father is a fundamental task that must be achieved in human development by all individuals to allow entry into the cultural and social realm through regulating human aggression and desire, sublimating sexuality and instituting the incest taboo.

[[RunningHead]]

Clients’ experiences of dynamic interpersonal therapy (DIT): opportunities and challenges for brief, manualised psychodynamic therapy in the NHS

Venetia Leonidaki , Alessandra Lemma and Imogen Hobbis

Imogen Hobbis

Norwich
UK
Department of Psychological Sciences, Norwich Medical School

London
UK

London
UK
Psychological Therapies Development Unit

Colchester
UK
School of Health and Human Sciences

Imogen Hobbis  

dynamic interpersonal therapy (DIT), brief psychodynamic psychotherapy, interpetative phenomenological analysis (IPA), qualitative research, clients’ experiences

Dynamic interpersonal therapy (DIT) is a brief manualised psychodynamic intervention for depression. This is a first study exploring clients’ experiences of DIT specifically and brief, manualised psychodynamic psychotherapy (PP) in general. Interpretative phenomenological analysis was the methodology employed. Five participants completed a semi-structured interview, three weeks to ten months after completing DIT. The scores of pre- and post-therapy outcome measures of depression and anxiety were also available. Two emerging superordinate themes are presented here: (1) ‘The Distinct Features of DIT’, referring to how its therapeutic style and time limitations were experienced and (2) the ‘Impact of Therapy’, referring to perceived outcomes. While previous findings showed that therapist’s perceived limited activity in long-term PP was experienced as hindering/unhelpful, the perceived sense of direction in DIT appeared adequate to most participants. Secondly, the time limitations provoked complex responses. Reactions to the distinct elements of DIT are to be treated both as therapeutic opportunities and as challenges. Further, in line with psychoanalytic theory, most participants described relational changes that went beyond symptom relief and remained in progress after therapy ended. Intriguingly, there was no consistency between participants’ qualitative accounts of change and the scores of the outcome measures.

responseSet.title: A new diagnosis of complex Post-traumatic Stress Disorder, PTSD – a window of opportunity for the treatment of patients in the NHS?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A new diagnosis of complex Post-traumatic Stress Disorder, PTSD – a window of opportunity for the treatment of patients in the NHS?

Catherine Matheson

Catherine Matheson

London
UK
Integrated Psychological Therapies Team, IPTT, Lewisham, South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trust

Catherine Matheson   *Email: c.matheson@nhs.net
complex PTSD, refugees, childhood sexual abuse, personality disorder, psychodynamic theory

The concept of complex trauma has been around for a long time and in 2018, it’s expected to become a new diagnosis in the International Classification of Diseases eleventh revision, ICD-11, the World Health Organisation, WHO, manual used formally in the NHS. Psychiatric diagnosis often does not sit well with psychoanalysis, which is at least as interested in unconscious phantasy as it is in symptoms. But as psychodynamically-trained practitioners in the NHS we need to engage with ICD-11 and apply our own understanding to service design so that patients have access to treatment which works for them. The service where I work (a secondary mental health team in the London borough of Lewisham) has already been receiving referrals for ‘complex trauma’ for some time, despite its not being formally classified. Patients so described are most often those with a history of childhood sexual abuse, and refugees with a history of brutality and torture. Differential diagnosis includes personality disorder since many have difficulties with interpersonal issues. In this paper I want to discuss how we might understand the new diagnosis of complex Post-traumatic Stress Disorder, PTSD, and its implications for treatment in the NHS.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A child’s loss becomes a young man’s violence

Carine Minne

Carine Minne

London
UK
The Portman Clinic

Carine Minne   *Email: CMinne@tavi-port.nhs.uk
forensic psychotherapy, personality disorders, psychosis

This paper describes the contribution of earlier traumatic loss to the later violent eruptions, with fatal consequences, of two young men, one who killed his mother and one who killed an unknown woman. Their developing personalities became disordered during their disturbed childhoods and the link between these developing personality disorders and psychoses is referred to. The need for long-term treatments is emphasised, as well as the importance of communication between therapist and clinical team.

[[RunningHead]]

Dynamic interpersonal therapy in an NHS tertiary level specialist psychotherapy service

Angela Douglas , Nicky Ablett-Tate and Nicola Chadd

Nicola Chadd

Stockton-on-Tees
UK
Specialist Psychotherapy Service

Nicola Chadd  

dynamic interpersonal therapy, NHS tertiary service, NHS specialist psychotherapy service, psychodynamic psychotherapy, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, payment by results

We describe the introduction of dynamic interpersonal psychotherapy (DIT) into an National Health Service (NHS) tertiary psychoanalytic specialist psychotherapy service. Training in DIT began as our contribution to Improving Access to Psychological Therapies and primary care services, supporting the training and supervision of their DIT practitioners. We then discovered DIT could be a valuable treatment within our own tertiary NHS service for patients with complex presentations. Currently fighting for survival, like many NHS psychoanalytic psychotherapy services nationally, we have adopted a manual-guided, psychoanalytically based therapy to broaden our tertiary clinical psychoanalytic service and accommodate trends in mental health service provision, whilst protecting the quality and integrity of our psychotherapy. DIT helped us continue providing relevant and beneficial psychoanalytic and psychodynamic services to individual patients despite limitations of the financially challenged NHS, NICE guidelines and Payment by Results. We outline the progress and outcomes for patients with complex mental health presentations, include individual case discussion and our experience of using the DIT approach within a traditionally longer term psychoanalytic psychotherapy service.

Understanding Dunblane and other massacres: forensic studies of homicide, paedophilia, and anorexia. Peter Aylward, Karnac Books, London (2012)

Eleanor Fellowes

Eleanor Fellowes

BSc Module Lead: Personality Disorder Knowledge and Understanding Framework (KUF)

Eleanor Fellowes   *Email: eleanorfellowes@gmail.com

In the Preface to this important book Peter Aylward invites you to hold in mind the experience of being both listener and reader, remembering that these chapters started as lectures. He suggests that the readers’ experience lacks an interpersonal dimension. Yet perhaps assisted by their original existence as lectures, Aylward’s authorial voice is animated and engaging and it does not feel as though the reader is being offered a diminished version. The opportunity to encounter these chapters in combination makes for fascinating reading, and the conviction and deftness with which they are written lends a fluency to subject matter which ordinarily defies understanding. And it is understanding that Aylward is asking for. Whilst the book grapples with complex ideas, and even more complex case material, its central plea is a simple one: that violence can and must be understood, even at its most extreme.

Tragedies will reoccur until we as a society are able to reach for true meaning, as opposed to conclusions based on prejudice and dyadic functioning, which replicates the perpetrator’s psychopathology. (p. 176)

His language of tragedy is not sentimentalised or hyperbolic. With rigorous attention to detail, Aylward traces the trans-generational histories of those who act out to catastrophic effect. Victims and perpetrators come to be seen as cast into roles not of their choosing, but performing a pre-written script with dire consequences. But Aylward is no determinist, and he is adamant that such scripts can be changed. The ideas he presents are not so much theories but tools, designed to advance society’s capacity to intervene and help those at risk of re-enacting the trauma of their early lives, and that which they have inherited from those around them.

Theoretically, the book’s starting points are various, but particularly prominent is the work of the Chilean psychoanalyst Matte Blanco (1908–1995). Clinically, they are Peter Aylward’s experiences as a forensic psychotherapist and supervisor, with a previous career in policing, primarily in anti-terrorism. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Making room for madness in mental health: the psychoanalytic understanding of psychotic communication

Alison Summers

Alison Summers

UK

Alison Summers   **@alison.summers77

Over the last twenty years or so, psychological aspects of psychosis have attracted renewed interest. The early intervention paradigm, developments in cognitive behavioural and family therapies, hearing voices networks, dialogical approaches, and research into attachment and to trauma have all played a part, but psychoanalytic contributions have been largely absent. It as if these recent developments and psychoanalytic thinking inhabit different worlds, and within mental health services it can feel as if a century of psychoanalytic attempts to understand psychosis had never happened. The situation is epitomised in Understanding Psychosis and Schizophrenia (Cooke, 2014), a major recent publication about psychological approaches to psychosis, where psychodynamic approaches are barely mentioned. In this context, Evans’ book seems to me to offer two very important contributions. Firstly it sets out what a psychoanalytic understanding might add, and secondly it makes a case for making use of such understanding.

In defining psychosis, Evans quotes Freud and O’Shaughnessy, viewing psychosis not as defined by psychiatric classifications, but in psychoanalytic terms, that is as a withdrawal from reality and thus a condition which may underlie apparently ‘neurotic’ problems, and which may at times affect us all. He includes chapters not only on conditions labelled psychosis within psychiatry, but also on borderline and antisocial personality, eating disorder, self harm and hysteria, arguing that recognising the psychotic processes that underlie such problems offers a better chance of understanding them. Despite this diagnostic breadth, the book seems to me very clearly focused on difficulties that are severe and challenging and which attract attention and resources even within the most hard-pressed mental health services. This aspect perhaps links to Evans’ own clinical experience, which is based not just on working therapeutically in specialist settings, but also on lengthy experience as a teacher and supervisor of front line mental health professionals and before this as a mental health nurse.

responseSet.title: The metapsychology of Christopher Bollas
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The metapsychology of Christopher Bollas

Heather Wood

Heather Wood

UK

Heather Wood   *Email: hwood@tavi-port.nhs.uk

In this slim volume, Sarah Nettleton provides a luminously clear and accessible overview of Chrisopher Bollas’ key psychoanalytic ideas. A renowned teacher and scholar of his work, she is well placed to offer such a useful guide.

The book is meticulously organised. Each chapter is devoted to a key concept such as ‘psychic dualities’, ‘idiom’, and ‘the unthought known’, and begins with a list of the key papers in which each concept is explored. In an appendix, there is a summary of selected papers. The index, in turn, is thorough. The reader can approach the subject from the direction of the individual concepts, or from browsing the papers that might be of interest.

There are many highlights. In Chapter 8 on ‘Unconscious Complexity’, Nettleton, herself a musician, describes Bollas’ notion that the elements of a psychoanalytic session resemble an orchestral score, in which there is both temporal movement from left to right on the page, and levels or threads of meaning, represented by the individual instruments, which may operate simultaneously and assume prominence at different times. In the analytic setting these threads might include ‘the patient’s narrative and its presentation; thoughts and feelings as they evolve in both patient and analyst; body expression; the diverse strands of the patient’s individual history and the shared history of the analytic work; the predominant transference relationship and other transient transference phenomena; …the projective use of the analytic object … and unconscious psychic structures that represent paradigms derived from early experience’ (pp. 56–57). The list is too long to be reproduced in its entirety, and Nettleton emphasises that it is still not comprehensive. The point to be stressed is that Bollas argues for an appreciation of the complexity and multiplicity of unconscious processes in operation at any one time, and moreover, that this complexity is a source of great richness and creativity. The following chapter, on ‘Free association’, is a reminder of the free associative method as a means of observing the unconscious connections between apparently unrelated phenomena. This is particularly evident in the ‘logic of sequence’, the apparent non-sequiturs of the analytic session. Nettleton draws out Bollas’ criticism of a psychoanalysis concerned only with symptoms and pathology, which essentially views the unconscious as a problem to be reckoned with, and highlights instead the pleasure that can derive from ‘the expansive experience of discovering new connections with the unconscious’ (p. 72).

[[RunningHead]]

Research on the Couch: Single-case studies, Subjectivity and Psychoanalytic Knowledge

Steve Bambrough

Steve Bambrough

London
UK
Associate Clinical Director in the Children Young Adults and Families Department

Steve Bambrough   *Email: sbambrough@tavi-port.nhs.uk

One does not have to venture far into this book to grasp that it has great implications and consequence for the practice of psychoanalysis. Hinshelwood’s stated view is that ‘Psychoanalysis must move into the business of testing’, meaning testing its theories. The book has as its focus, the integration of two positions which are often in conflict within psychoanalytic literature: (a) is psychoanalysis a hermeneutic science? or (b) is it an empirical natural science? The author’s method is also absolutely clear – he returns to clinical psychoanalytic practice as the foundation of psychoanalytic knowledge and also the site of its examination and testing.

Hinshelwood’s book is an attempt to defend psychoanalysis as a ‘rigorous and evidenced body of knowledge’ (p. 2) and the rigour and thoroughness in his investigation is remarkable. It is also intended to give us a research method by which to objectively compare ‘the cacophony of differing views’ and methods within the analytic tradition. As you may have grasped thus far, the task Hinshelwood has set himself in this volume is a daunting one.

The book itself is divided into 22 chapters in 4 parts. The first part, covering 35 pages, sets the scene for some of the key challenges which psychoanalytic research has to encounter: the subjectivity of the field of observation; the problem of generalising from single-case studies; whether inductive thinking can be used to establish a scientific theory; and the problem of data collecting. It reviews social science approaches to the study of psychoanalytic theory and shows the ‘growing hegemony of “empirical” process research’ (p. 31) in contrast to an approach which sees the clinical process as the research process itself. He describes this latter position as giving back to the clinician the possibility ‘once more’ (p. 30) of joining up the actual work with a research perspective, and this is the position Hinshelwood supports, in pursuit of a ‘form of on-couch process research’. He is also clear that he believes psychoanalysis cannot duck these questions which are fundamental to addressing what kind of knowledge it possesses.

Medical psychotherapy consultation: psychoanalytic psychiatry for the patient and professional

James Johnston , Vikram Luthra , Lackson Mzizi and Alastair Cardno

Alastair Cardno

Leeds
UK
Senior Lecturer in Psychiatry

Leeds
UK

Leeds
UK
Specialty Trainee in General Adult Psychiatry (ST5)

Leeds
UK

Alastair Cardno  

medical psychotherapy, psychoanalytic consultation, countertransference, dynamic field, being stuck, adhesive identification, the last bastion of the patient and professional

An NHS Mental Health Trust Medical Psychotherapy Consultation Service using psychoanalytic psychiatry to help the patient and professional is described. The Consultation Service established in 2000 is offered to secondary acute and community mental health teams and primary care. The service was evaluated as a basis for regional and national development. Between 2006 and 2013, 87 consultations from 210 were sampled to ascertain demographic and diagnostic profiles and outcomes of the consultation process. We conducted an online survey of local consultant psychiatrists’ views about the service, and undertook a thematic analysis of the free text comments. We also conducted a survey of members of the Royal College of Psychiatrists’ Medical Psychotherapy Faculty to ascertain whether similar consultation services existed elsewhere in the UK and had been evaluated. The Leeds model of psychoanalytic consultation – a ‘consultation sandwich’ – is described. From a psychoanalytic perspective, the work of consultation is seen as an extension of the dynamic field of the analytic situation. This paper develops the concept of a bastion – an omnipotent reserve in and between the patient and professional derived from adhesive identifications leading to stuck relationships. The adhesive identification in the patient and professional acts like a ‘grievance glue’ – a mutual manifestation in a last bastion of painful limitations not faced, losses not grieved.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Can psychoanalysis help us to understand the causes and prevention of violence?

James Gilligan

James Gilligan

New York, NY
USA
Department of Psychiatry, School of Medicine

James Gilligan   *Email: james.gilligan@nyu.edu
Shame, guilt, violence, extinction, morality, psychoanalysis, science, public health, preventive medicine

As the most adequate theory of human personality yet created, including our propensity for irrational, self-defeating, and self-destructive behavior patterns, psychoanalysis is in a unique position to help us solve the most serious crisis that the human species has been confronted by at any point in its evolutionary history: the threat to its own survival that is caused by its own behavior, namely the compulsion to engage in violence on the largest scale that its technology makes possible, even when that causes the death of the self as well as of others. This continually expanding behavior pattern, which reduces even genocide to a minor footnote compared with the self-extinction of our whole species, is created not only by “apocalyptic” fundamentalism and terrorism (“suicide bombers”), but also by the increasing and only partially preventable proliferation of thermonuclear weapons to national rulers of questionable sanity, and the apparently unpreventable continuation of industrial/economic policies and practices that will, if not reversed, make our own small planet uninhabitable. What makes people place a higher value on the continuation of these behaviors than they place on their own physical survival (or that of their children)? That is the question to which this article proposes at least the beginnings of an answer: that when a person feels shamed and humiliated to a degree that threatens the survival of that fragile and vulnerable psychological construct called his “self” (or of the religious or cultural group with which his self has identified), he will eagerly sacrifice his body (and other peoples’) in the attempt to, as he sees it, save his soul, i.e. his self and his self-esteem. The question then becomes: what are the social and psychological determinants of overwhelming shame and humiliation, and how can we protect people from being exposed to those conditions, or at least enhance their ability to respond to them in ways that are life-preserving rather than life-destroying?

[[RunningHead]]

A fertile matrix: the birth of the Forensic Psychotherapy Society

Gabriel Kirtchuk , John Gordon , Ronald Doctor and Richard Ingram

Richard Ingram

Isleworth
UK
Psychotherapy Service

Belfast
UK
Centre for Psychotherapy

London
UK
Forensic Psychotherapy Department

Richard Ingram  

Forensic psychotherapy, training, medical psychotherapy, consultation, supervision

This paper traces some aspects of the fertile matrix within which, over many years, the application of a psychoanalytic perspective to clinical work with the most severely mentally ill, difficult and at times frightening patients in secure and community settings has evolved and now led to the recent inauguration of the Forensic Psychotherapy Society (FPS), a new Member Institution of the British Psychoanalytic Council. The Society provides both a framework for a national, multidisciplinary professional training in psychodynamic forensic psychotherapy and the potential launching pad for an active post-qualification organisation supporting CPD and all other forms of professional development. The particular contributions to the birth of the FPS of the Forensic Psychotherapy Department at West London Mental Health NHS Trust, partly because we know it best but also because it is part of the largest forensic psychiatric service in the United Kingdom, are described.

[[RunningHead]]

Pluralistic approaches to the study of process and outcome in psychoanalysis. The LAC depression study: a case in point

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber , Lisa Kallenbach and Margerete Schoett

Margerete Schoett

Kassel
Germany

Frankfurt am Main
Germany

Margerete Schoett  

clinical and extra-clinical research in psychoanalysis, plurality of methods, theories and research, outcome and process research in psychoanalysis, chronic depression

Approaching process and outcome in psychoanalysis is a topic that touches on ambitious and complex epistemological and methodological issues. As discussed in the first part of the paper, in keeping with challenging epistemic considerations, it would seem appropriate to describe the specificity of psychoanalysis as a specific scientific discipline of the unconscious (spezifische Wissenschaft des Unbewussten, see LIT). Psychoanalysis, over its more than 100-year history, evolved a range of highly advanced research methods for investigating the specific object of its research, namely unconscious conflicts and fantasies. Hence, like many other scientific disciplines, contemporary psychoanalysis comprises a plurality of theories, methods of clinical treatments as well as a plurality of research topics. This position is discussed with reference to a model illustrating different forms of clinical and extra-clinical research in psychoanalysis. A major, ongoing comparative study of outcomes of psychoanalytical and cognitive behavioural, long-term treatments of chronically depressed patients, the so-called LAC-study, serves to illustrate the richness of contemporary research in psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Making minds more secure: remembering Gill McGauley
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Making minds more secure: remembering Gill McGauley

Gwen Adshead

Gwen Adshead

Gwen Adshead  

Forensic psychiatry and psychotherapy lost a major voice and thinker in July 2016 when Professor Gill McGauley died suddenly and unexpectedly, only three weeks after she had been diagnosed with cancer of the oesophagus. This was a cruel and devastating loss; and especially painful because her family, friends and colleagues in the field of forensic psychotherapy had rejoiced with her in May when she gave her inaugural address as Professor of Medical Education and Forensic Psychotherapy at St George’s Hospital, London.

These types of loss are hard to articulate, or make sense of: there is a terrible irony here that Gill herself would be the best person to help us think about it. As many will know, Gill had made a particular study of attachment theory as it applied to forensic psychiatry, and had carried out important research into the childhood histories and attachment representations of violent offenders with severe personality disorders. Together with Luisa Avellan, she helped us to think about how a disorganized attachment system might have devastating effects on the capacity to be pro-social and to mentalize distress in adulthood. She was a member of an active group at Broadmoor Hospital, which sought to explore how her research, and that of Bateman and Fonagy (2008), Bateman, O’Connell, Lorenzini, Gardner, and Fonagy (2016) could be applied to the development of therapeutic interventions for men and women who were not only high risk offenders, but also suffered mental illness and personality disorder.

Gill was kind and funny and clever; wearing her learning lightly, and also managing to handle with grace and patience the distressing material we work with everyday as forensic psychotherapists. She would be the first to say that she was supported in this work by her family’s love and care, and also by her time as a patient in psychoanalysis. The psychoanalytic frame of reference helped her maintain that ‘third position’ that is crucial to forensic work, both in individual work, and at the organizational level. She was a valued member of the reflective practice team at Broadmoor, which tried to support and enable ward staff to care for forensic patients in long stay care; a job which is hard because it is complex, and emotionally laborious.

responseSet.authorCitation: Negele, A., Kaufhold, J. & Leuzinger-Bohleber, M.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Childhood trauma from a patient and a psychoanalyst perspective: linking chronic depression to relational multiple trauma

Alexa Negele , Johannes Kaufhold and Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber

Frankfurt
Germany

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber  

chronic depression, childhood trauma, multiple childhood trauma, relational trauma, patient ratings, psychoanalyst perspectives

The relationship between multiple childhood trauma, as well as adversity, and chronic depression has been reported repeatedly. However, there is a lack of clinical differentiations of these findings. We complemented patient self-ratings, using the Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ), with psychoanalysts’ perspectives in order to provide finer grained clinical differentiations of the trauma behind chronic depression. These differentiations connect the trauma scales with early relational experiences. We developed a bespoke instrument derived from psychoanalytic trauma concepts. A subsample of 52 cases of chronically depressed patients alongside their 24 psychoanalysts was taken from the LAC depression study, in order to complement patient and psychoanalyst ratings. Our results confirm the connection between multiple childhood trauma and chronic depression. Besides relational trauma, the psychoanalysts’ perspective found separation trauma and transgenerational transmission of trauma to be significant. These traumatic relationships seem to precede and accompany adverse life events and/or traumatic experiences. They may even prevent adequate coping and/or processing of such experiences. Patient interview material from study intake and five-year follow-up further provides an insight into the changes the trauma narratives undergo throughout time. These changes emerged due to a joint reconstruction of the meaning of traumatic experiences throughout the course of the psychoanalytic process.

responseSet.title: Revisiting the crossroads: can Oedipus take a gay turn in the 21st C?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revisiting the crossroads: can Oedipus take a gay turn in the 21st C?

Jeremy Clarke

Jeremy Clarke

London
UK
Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Jeremy Clarke   *Email: therapy@practice.demon.co.uk
the law of desire, perversion, internalized stigma, triangular space, coming out

Is our foundational story of sexual identity still relevant and valid for today, or is it wrong? Oedipus has been used in the past as a cautionary tale for the consequences of transgressive sexuality, as well as an exemplar of hetero-normative development. Perhaps most influentially he has been used recently to illustrate a mechanism that underpins the concept of a pathological organization of personality, a perverse turning of a blind eye to the truth. But is this reading mistaken? In this article I return to the crossroads with Oedipus to try to give him back – for the first time – his sexual identity. By offering a re-reading of the myth in light of how we understand the impact of internalized stigma on the formation of sexual identity today I hope to show that our psychoanalytic imagination can use the Oedipus myth to encompass a range of different developmental possibilities.

responseSet.title: In praise of Dr Leslie Sohn 1919–2013
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In praise of Dr Leslie Sohn 1919–2013

Carine Minne

Carine Minne

Carine Minne  

Dr Leslie Sohn, a world expert on the psychotic mind, an NHS psychiatrist for 62 years, a psychoanalyst (training adult and child analyst), a collector of Chinese antiquities and a lover of flowers, a lifelong supporter of Manchester United FC, a family man, died on 18 March 2013 aged 93 years. Dr Sohn spent his incredibly long working life dedicated to the plight of patients in forensic psychiatry systems and teaching and supervising professionals from the different disciplines involved in their care.

Leslie Sohn was born in Cape Town, 1919, the youngest of three children, having two sisters several years older. He attended the local public day school and then read medicine at the University of Cape Town. His attachment to the psychiatric department of the university hospital produced clear signs of a profound interest in this field. He completed his House Officer posts in Durban and waited for the war to end before coming to England for postgraduate training in psychiatry.

Dr Sohn was delighted to be appointed Senior House Physician in 1947 to Aubrey Lewis at the Maudsley Hospital. In 1948, with the forming of the National Health Service, his attachment became one of Psychiatric Registrar and then, Senior Registrar. The word ‘psychoanalysis’ had not yet entered his vocabulary. Whilst working at the Maudsley Hospital, he was fortunate to work clinically for Clifford Scott and William Gillespie who inspired his psychoanalytic interest. He was encouraged to apply to the Institute of Psychoanalysis, was accepted and supervised by Marion Milner and Hannah Segal, qualifying in 1952. Two of his peers during the training were Sidney Klein and Isabelle Menzies-Lyth with whom he maintained a friendship over several decades. They continued to meet regularly at postgraduate seminars, including over a period of 12 years at the Rosenfeld seminar, where Sohn and Klein presented. A similar pattern took place at the Segal postgraduate seminar over many years.

Throughout this time, Dr Sohn continued working at the Maudsley Hospital and, post-qualification as a psychoanalyst, began to develop his private practice. He also completed the Child Psychoanalytic training.

responseSet.title: Creating mother and baby therapy groups in prison: emotional valuation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creating mother and baby therapy groups in prison: emotional valuation

Pamela Windham Stewart

Pamela Windham Stewart

London
UK

Pamela Windham Stewart   *Email: Pjwindham@aol.com
mothers, pregnancy, prison, group therapy, forensic psychotherapy

Mothers in prison are at high risk of depression and other mental health issues and yet access to therapeutic services is limited. This paper describes the development by the author of two prison therapy groups: one for pregnant women, the other for mothers and their babies. Called the ‘Born Inside’ project, both groups are currently held in Bronzefield Prison, Surrey. The theory and principles behind the project are discussed, as well as the processes and practicalities involved in providing the therapy and specialised support to the women and their vulnerable infants. In addition, principles of forensic psychotherapy in relation to this specific prison population are described, and the situational and emotional challenges faced are illustrated through narratives of group members.

responseSet.authorMast: Marco Chiesa,  Martina Larsen-Paya,  Maria Martino and  Martina Trinchieri
responseSet.authorCitation: Chiesa, M., Larsen-Paya, M., Martino, M. & Trinchieri, M.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The relationship between childhood adversity, psychiatric disorder and clinical severity: results from a multi-centre study

Marco Chiesa , Martina Larsen-Paya , Maria Martino and Martina Trinchieri

Martina Trinchieri

London
UK
Clinical, Educational & Health Psychology

Richmond
UK

Martina Trinchieri  

childhood adversity, personality disorder, clinical severity, predictive study, multi-center study

Although several studies have shown a clear association between childhood adversities and later development of a number of psychiatric disorders, a number of recent reports have cast doubts regarding the aetiological role played by abuse, neglect and loss, arguing that they are neither necessary nor sufficient to account for the development of subsequent psychopathology. In this study, we aimed to investigate the associations and predictive power of childhood adversities on later onset of Axis-I &-II psychiatric conditions and the impact on severity of psychiatric presentation in a large sample of patients. This cross-sectional multi-centre study evaluated patients (N = 1136) recruited in fourteen NHS psychotherapy departments in the UK. Patients were assessed using a number of rater-based questionnaires and self-rated measures. Multiple regression analyses showed that childhood experiences of loss and physical abuse were predictive of severe personality disorder and borderline personality disorder, but not of other less severe PD and Axis-I diagnoses. Sexual abuse predicted later onset of Post-traumatic Stress Disorder. In addition, we found that childhood abusive experiences were strongly associated with severity of psychiatric distress, interpersonal problems and global functioning. Having experienced two or more childhood adversities significantly increased severity of psychiatric distress, interpersonal malfunctioning and CORE scores.

[[RunningHead]]

Core Competencies in Brief Dynamic Psychotherapy – Becoming a Highly Effective and Competent Brief Dynamic Psychotherapist

Jonathan Garabette

Jonathan Garabette

Berkshire
England
Locum Consultant Psychiatrist in Forensic Psychiatry and Medical Psychotherapy

Jonathan Garabette   *Email: jonathan.garabette@wlmht.nhs.uk

I approached this book under a misapprehension. Having recently completed my medical (psychodynamic) psychotherapy training within an NHS setting in which longer term treatments were rare to come by and in which 16 sessions of brief psychodynamic psychotherapy was often the limit with even severely unwell patients, I was seeking further guidance on how best to maximise every moment of this time. I was attracted therefore by this book, whose title promised to deliver wisdom regarding the ‘core competencies’ in brief dynamic psychotherapy, and even on ‘becoming a highly effective and competent Brief Dynamic Psychotherapist’. I was therefore a little taken aback when I discovered that the ‘Brief Dynamic Psychotherapy’ in the title was in fact referring to a very particular manualised model of psychotherapy previously developed and described by one of the authors (Binder, 2004). I was somewhat reluctant to read on, as I already felt at saturation point with three-letter acronym models of therapy (CBT,CAT, DBT, TFT, MBT, DIT and so on) and didn’t particularly want to digest the manual of another. However, I’m glad that I did, for the authors set out their integrative model with clarity and detail. Those looking for a checklist of competencies with detailed breakdown of the requirements for different levels of proficiency will not find this here. Instead, the ‘core competencies’ are gradually discussed throughout the text in an integrated manner as the authors go through what they consider the key components of the therapeutic process. I found this interesting and genuinely thought provoking in helping me reflect upon the process and rationale of my own practice, both in brief and longer term psychodynamic psychotherapy cases.

In terms of the book’s structure, after a chapter on the definition, development and importance of competencies in general, and a preview of competency areas in BDP, the authors go on to provide a conceptual overview of their BDP model. They then progress through the components of the BDP process in a systematic fashion, from ‘forming an effective therapeutic alliance’ to ‘planning for termination and maintaining treatment gains’. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Don’t tell me I’m normal: inpatient psychotherapy of a severely abused patient at the Cassel Hospital

John Glyn

John Glyn

Ham
UK

John Glyn   *Corresponding author. Email: jglyn@tiscali.co.uk
Cassel Hospital, culture of enquiry, personality disorder, applied psychoanalysis

This paper is a case study of a patient with severe personality disorder undergoing treatment at the Cassel Hospital. It looks at a phase of her individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy and how that sits within an institutional model of treatment that can be understood as applied psychoanalysis, in which Tom Main’s concept of a ‘culture of enquiry’ aims to observe and bring together splitting and projective processes operating at all levels of the organisation. The patient, who had been sexually abused and then suffered a prolonged suicidal breakdown, exerted intense pressure on the institution through her experience of herself as both a victim of abuse and a participant in it. A working through is described, in which different parts of the patient that had been projected into different parts of the institution are brought together, and her primitive demand for a two-person world gives way to an acceptance of triangulation. Themes from her individual sessions are enacted in the milieu-based treatment, and conversely struggles in the patient community appear in her sessions. It is from drawing these parts of the patient together as a whole institution that the treatment derives its power and effectiveness.

[[RunningHead]]

Social defences against anxiety: explorations in a paradigm

Arabella Kurtz

Arabella Kurtz

UK

Arabella Kurtz   *Email: ak106@le.ac.uk

One of the main sources of the social defence paradigm, the subject of Armstrong and Rustin’s edited book, is Isabel Menzies Lyth’s paper from 1960, which describes her ground-breaking study of counter-therapeutic dynamics in a hospital nursing service (Menzies Lyth, 1960). I first read this seminal work a few years into my career as a clinical psychologist working in adult and forensic mental health services in the NHS. It created such a strong sense of recognition in me of the sometimes paradoxical and disturbing interactions I observed around me, both between staff and in staff’s dealings with patients, that I wished I had read it much sooner. As a result, I have given the paper as set reading to clinical psychology trainees now for over a decade, and the continued relevance of Menzies Lyth’s observations and her concept of the social defence system is regularly confirmed for me by their enthusiastic responses.

The contributors to ‘Social Defences Against Anxiety: Explorations in a Paradigm’, also pay homage to the enduring explanatory power of the social defence paradigm. The volume originated in a symposium at St John’s College in 2013, the aim of which was to breathe new life into the model by considering it in the light of developments in theory and practice, both within the world of psychoanalysis and in the context of broader social and cultural changes. The book consists of an introductory chapter by the editors, which describes the advances in psychoanalytic thinking that most influence the papers that follow, as well as salient aspects of the changes in the nature of institutions and in the wider environment, particularly the development of global communications and the global market economy. The articles are organised into four sections; the first is theoretical, and the following three collect applications of Menzies Lyth’s ideas in the fields of healthcare, the private sector, and social work and education, respectively.

The papers vary in terms of the quality of the writing and the extent to which they succeed in integrating practice with contemporary theory. But overall, this seems to me a very important and interesting book. It is important because it repeatedly confirms the unique place of psychoanalysis in furthering our understanding of the social world, perhaps especially in beginning to describe the complexity of our experience of the changes to personal and group identity involved in neoliberalism and the global market economy. [[RunningHead]]

The topic of cancer: new perspectives on the emotional experience of cancer

John Lawrence

John Lawrence

Psycho-analyst

John Lawrence   *Email: john@jarndyce.net

Perhaps unintentionally, the editor of this book, Jonathan Burke sets the basis for debate between, on the one hand, authors offering psychoanalytically based psychotherapy or counselling to cancer patients and, on the other hand, those who regard the cancer as primary and advocate that counsellors need to be informed about the illness to assist patients effectively.

The psychoanalytically oriented authors represented here believe that they should not offer information (often regarding it as ‘teaching’), much less offer reassurance, which they see as avoiding a patient’s feelings. They regard their role as revealing unconscious processes and assisting patients through understanding. Those favouring a disease-focused approach believe it important, not only to provide relevant understanding of patients’ feelings, but also to provide information, appropriate reassurance, and support for patients to become as informed about their illness as they may need or wish to be.

Two chapters, ‘On Survivorship’ and ‘Palliative Care’ offer a disease-focused approach, providing advice about information that patients need and indicating what knowledge and awareness counsellors require to better help cancer patients. Other chapters, written from a psychoanalytic viewpoint, are less concerned with cancer per se, as they are with illustrating how a psychoanalytic approach informs their style of counselling or psychotherapy with cancer patients.

If there is a debate, then what can each side can learn from the other? This volume does not raise that question, but rather shows that, if anything, at present, there is limited traffic between the psychoanalytic theory-based workers and those who take a more illness and evidence-led approach.

A recent research study shows how important bridging this gap is. Caroline Kamau’s (2015) study ‘Preparing patients with cancer who work and treatment responsiveness’ suggests that patients can be helped towards a better cancer outcome if their carers offer useful information about the illness. From my experience as a cancer patient, counselling during illness or post-illness is likely to be more relevant if the counsellor knows about the cancer and its effects. Counsellors and psychotherapists may object that information about cancer should, indeed, can only be given by treating doctors. But doctors are not always able or willing to provide their patients with information, and patients may need support to seek it, including obtaining further medical opinion. [[RunningHead]]

The relationship between personality organization and sexual life in a community sample of men

Antonio Prunas , Rossella Di Pierro and Roberto Bernorio

Roberto Bernorio

Milan
Italy

Milan
Italy
Department of Psychology

Roberto Bernorio  

men’s sexual health, adult sexuality, sexual dysfunctions, sexual satisfaction, paraphilia, borderline personality

Contemporary object-relation theory of personality postulates that the level of severity of personality organization parallels the nature and extent of problems in the patient’s sexual life. The study aims at exploring the relationship between dimensions of Borderline Personality Organization (BPO) (as assessed according to Otto Kernberg’s model), sexual functioning, quality of sexual life and paraphilias in a community sample of men. One-hundred and thirty-six healthy men were asked to complete a set of questionnaires including the Inventory of Personality Organization (IPO), the International Index of Erectile Function, the Sexual Quality of Life Questionnaire, and a checklist to assess the prevalence of paraphilias. High scores on IPO subscales were significantly associated with low overall satisfaction in sexual functioning, low quality of sexual life, and the presence of paraphilias. Results expand previous findings on the relationship between features of BPO and core components of sexual life and support the need for an assessment of personality functioning in subjects reporting sexual problems.

responseSet.title: The good story: exchanges on truth, fiction and psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The good story: exchanges on truth, fiction and psychotherapy

Gabrielle Brown

Gabrielle Brown

London
UK

Gabrielle Brown   *Email: gbrown@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This is a fascinating and unusual book, which debates the moral status of literature, psychotherapy and the individual’s ethical position within their historical milieu. It presents a series of e-mail exchanges between a psychotherapist and a writer. Arabella Kurtz is a psychoanalytic psychotherapist and Consultant Clinical Psychologist, working and teaching in mental health in England. J.M. Coetzee is a Nobel and Booker Prize winning novelist. Both are deeply steeped in Western literature and myth – Dostoyevsky, Sebald, classical Greek mythology, Hawthorne, Freud – which stimulates and illustrates their exchange. Kurtz also introduces her correspondent to psychoanalytic ideas in the tradition of Klein and Winnicott, as well as the theories of groups and social systems from Menzies Lyth and Bion. Each chapter is headed by a summary of the issues under debate. I particularly enjoyed the glossary of psychoanalytic concepts which Kurtz provides, in which important terms are beautifully rendered into accessible common language.

Coetzee is interested in the relationship between storytelling and concepts of telling ‘the truth’. Early on he asks ‘do we trust the human imagination as an invariable force for good?’ (p. 15) and later ‘about every story we can legitimately ask cui bono’ (p. 60). For Kurtz, what is at stake in fictions of the self is how psychic mechanisms, such as repression and the repetition compulsion, prevent an individual from accepting ‘the invitation to inhabit one’s perspective – to understand and own it for all its difficulty and its complexity in as mindful a way as possible’ (p. 17). She counters Coetzee’s image of a human drive towards deliberate self-deception with accounts of uncovering unconscious communication through free association, play, symbolisation and group reflection in clinical work. With great subtlety she shows how psychoanalysis’ concept of unconscious processes unsettles ideas of intentionality and culpability. As Menzies Lyth commented in describing consulting to organisations: ‘people do not say what they really mean even when they honestly and sincerely say what they consciously think, let alone when they do not’ (1989, pp. 28, 29). From this perspective, juridical notions of the ‘truth of the matter’ do not give the full story.
